speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number five. I'm speaking with Mrs. Magda Klein. Today is July 8, 1996. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Mrs. Klein, you were just telling me that you realized the man doing some of the selection was Dr. Mengele. What else can you tell me about him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he came quite often to select us because being that we stayed in Auschwitz from-- like almost the middle or from end of May until end of November, which very few people who stayed that length of a time in Auschwitz. So unfortunately, we had encountered his selections many, many times. There were times that he was selecting for the skinny ones. And I would try to perfect my sister. I would always look at a skinny girl when she was going. That's when I would put my sister after her. I figure that Mengele would select her so then my sister would slide through, and it-- many times it worked.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what Mengele was selecting for?

speaker: interviewee
text: By that time, we were so experienced. We know that when he's selecting the skinny ones, that goes to the crematorium. When he's-- there was this old man whom they claim that that's Mengele's father. He came and he spoke perfect Hungarian. So that's-- I-- I don't know if he was or not. But among us, it was that that's Grandpa Mengele. And he said perfect Hungarian, my dear children, I'm a doctor. And whoever has any problem, any pain, any sickness, you just come over to me and we will cure you and we will make you well enough, and you should have faith in us. Meantime, the Blockalteste had warned us that these things will happen. And if you have a toothache or if you have a stomachache, it doesn't mean you're ready for the crematorium. So do not volunteer that you will be taken to a beautiful hospital and you will be given food, because you will not.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when this old man, elderly gentleman in the uniform, and he spoke perfect Hungarian, and he said that we should volunteer and so on, if you are sick. There was a handful who didn't believe what the Blockalteste said, and they volunteered. But it wasn't that many volunteer.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was another time when there was this other German female officer, and they came and they selected all the young looking girls. So they said that they going to form a barrack that all the children will be. So that time, I didn't mind. I let my children-- I let my sister Elizabeth to be selected. So they put them in the block 20-- I mean the barrack 22.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one morning, I see that the German officer and is marching them to the end of the lager. And as I'm watching, she was making them do exercise. Up and down and this and this and this and this. So I said, I give out that little fat what he has-- what she has on. She going to lose it because how long can she-- can you keep on exercising without food and without all that? So next day, I said to her as they were marching back, I hollered at her. And I said after Zahlappell, come to block so-and-so. Because to tell you the honest things that what happened is that somehow I had a feeling when they coming to select, and I would go over to another block to-- to stay at Zahlappell. So what happened is that after every morning, we would stand Zahlappell in a-- a-- a different barrack, different block. So I told my sister where to come, and then she came. And when we went up that night to lay in the-- the [POLISH], the bunk bed, some of the girls said that she doesn't belong in here. She-- she's a child.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was-- and she came from the children-- so I told her to shut you mouth and so and so. And she kept quiet. There was another occasion where we were selected, my sister Clara and myself. And my cousin [? Alice ?] and my sister Elizabeth, they stayed behind.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they locked us up in one of the block, one of the barrack. And we were standing there, staying there. And I'm looking, and the-- the edge of the-- the-- the barrack was full of bread, which meant that one of the Stubenalteste who stole the bread because somebody died and this was extra portion, and they put it up there. So I started eating. And I'm sitting on-- up on top of the-- the barn. And my sister Elizabeth is standing, walking back and forth, and she is crying. And she said, you come out. If not, I'm going to go against the-- the fence because I don't want to live without the two of you, and so on. So I said, here is a bread. And I throw out one bread, with another bread, another bread. I said, eat it and stop complaining. Stop crying. We will get out. We will get-- now she said, I-- I want you to come out. I don't want to stay this way. I don't want your lousy so-and-so bread.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said to Clara. I said, you know, she doesn't want a bread. And she is carrying on something terrible. I want to make sure that you break out. I don't want to be alone here. So I said, soon as I have a chance, we will do it. And it was quiet for a while. Then I heard a big commotion. And we go to the front-- not the front. To the back of the barrack, and there was a Stubenalteste who was put there to guard the door, that nobody should go out. And let me tell you, those Stubenalteste, they were like [HUNGARIAN]. That's how strong and that's how fed up they were.

speaker: interviewee
text: So outside, there was another Stubenalteste who was standing there. So some of the girls whose mother was inside and the girl was outside or the sister was inside, the sister was outside. So they ran over to the Stubenalteste. They started kicking and biting. And they pushed her away from the door. They opened the-- the [? leash ?].

speaker: interviewee
text: And they ran in. And they were pushing and shoving. Some of them stayed inside. Some of them went outside. So we went outside. And when I was going out, I don't know. I pushed my sister Clara. But when I get to my turn, I got like stationary. I-- I was stone.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the Stubenalteste started to grab me, to get me back. And I had on a-- a very nice little pullover. Not the same what I got the first time. And she took off a sleeve, the way she grab me. And then we went out.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we went out, there was a big Zahlappell. The whole area, everybody was seated on the ground. So when we went there, we went hurriedly and we sat down with the other girls. So later on, the Lageralteste came and says that [INAUDIBLE] there are girls missing from inside. I need some girls.

speaker: interviewee
text: So one of the [HUNGARIAN], they spoke Jewish. And we didn't speak Jewish. She started to say something. So my sister took the knife and says if you open up your big filthy so-and-so mouth, I'm going to kill you before you have a chance to say boo. So she says, she's mashugana, she's mashugana. So I said, oh, yeah. She is mashugana. You better listen to her. So we sat there. We got the fifth one. And we were there.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were times when they selected us. We were lucky we survive. And each time when they selected is like 100 years fell off our shoulders.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you stay healthy, or did you get ill?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was just getting to it. And there was a time when my younger sister got this rash. And when Mengele selected, she-- he wanted only skin-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] clean skin. No rash. No blemish. No cuts. Clean.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when we had the lice eating us up, we would shove it off. We wouldn't dare to scratch. As much as it hurt and much as it was itching, we wouldn't dare to scratch. So when my sister got this rash, there was-- I didn't even know about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she knew about it, that there was a place end of the lager where they had some kind of a ointment, and they put it in your body, that cleaned out the-- it's a infection that spreads like wildfire. They call it [HUNGARIAN] in Hungary, But I don't know what's the name in English. And as we were standing Zahlappell, was hot like anything, and my sister Clara is throwing up only green saliva. And she said, I'm fainting. I can't stand the smell of it. Whoever it is, get out. And this and that. So my sister Elizabeth says, stop shouting. Stop cursing. I'm the one who has it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, then why didn't you tell us? I didn't want to tell you. I still don't know why-- you know what, meantime, my cousin [? Alice ?], every time she had a chance she was going towards the fence. She just wanted to get to the fence. She wanted to commit suicide.

speaker: interviewee
text: Every time, I was running after her because then the people get to know you already, because there was new-- there was no more new transport coming in. So it was more like the station, and this always was getting less and less because they were taken to the crematorium or they were taken to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened is that this-- no, I get confused. I don't know what I was saying. So-- I really don't know where I was-- You were going to tell me about illness. Did you stay healthy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. So what happened is that one day of-- out of nowhere I became so weak. I had such a diarrhea that I just couldn't control it. I was trying to run. By the time I would run, it was already on my leg. And when I got off the bunk bed, I was already-- I mean, it-- maybe it was just water, but I thought-- I was very, very weak. So we were standing Zahlappell. My sister Clara was front, and she was holding me with the other hand in the back. My sister Elizabeth, she was holding me from the front on my back.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when they were counting the Zahlappell, that's how I did it. Then I went over to one of the woman who I know that during the night, she is cooking for the Blockaltestes. Because every time we would go to sleep in our bunk bed, there was, let's say, five board. In a short period of time, there was only three. So can you see yourself laid down crosswise on three board that the one part is sticking out there because they were busy breaking it off to-- to cook on it for the Blockaltestes. So I go over to her. I get off the bunk bed during the night and I go over to her.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, look. Here is my bread. Throw it on the-- on the fire or give me a piece of-- of coal so that I could eat it to bind me up. I said, or please put my bread on the fire so that it should burn so that I should burn.

speaker: interviewee
text: She started cursing. You get out of here. If the Blockaltestes see you, I will be dead and you will be dead. What do you want from me? Why do you do that to me? I said to her, look. I said, if you have a heart, I said, why can't should do that? I mean, there's nobody in here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then she grabbed the bread. She throw it in there. It was there a little short time. She says, well, disappear and go away from me. Do not even stand near, but go away. And then slowly, slowly I started to get stronger. I started to feel better. But it took a lot out of me. There was another occasion where we were-- we were standing Zahlappell, and this German soldier, this, was counting. And as he is counting, some of the girls, they just couldn't hold the urine that long because their bladder was very big. And he is counting with his stick. And then he sees the urine on the ground. Because from all that trapping from that legs, you know, as you are walking and standing on it, the earth became like rocks. That's how hard it was. So she couldn't scrape up a little earth to cover up the urine.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he said, 'Was ist das?' You know, German. So she doesn't answer. Then again. So then he goes and he hits her the most sensitive spot in the-- in the ear. And then he hits her on the other ear. And then she doesn't say anything. So he said, you were too lazy to go to the bathroom. Aren't you ashamed of yourself, you urine on there yourself? So the girl doesn't say anything. And then again he hits her, and she falls down because we were like flies. So weak. So she falls down. And then to make sure for good measure, he makes another hit. And then he smiles and he said, she is tired. She wants to go to sleep.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, this is just-- just part of it. And you had asked me what we were doing in Auschwitz. I went over to the kitchen, and they had dumped a big truckload of cabbage. And I see that the-- all the Polish kitchen workers, they all stand around. And I mean, they all sitting, and they are cleaning the cabbage. What they were cleaning, I don't know. They were cutting it. I don't know what they were doing because I wasn't interested about what they were doing. The only thing I know, they were seated around the-- the cabbage. And there was this big husky German woman, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] how they called the woman's-- whatever it is. I can't think of the name of it now. [GERMAN]. She is standing there with her legs stretched. And I figured out if I crawl between her leg-- where I get the nerve, I don't know. Don't ask me. I crawled between her leg very finely, and I grabbed the cabbage. Meantime, all these Polish girls who were seated there, their mouth open. And they just didn't want to believe it, that is such a thing possible. So I figured, just keep your mouth open long enough until I will-- so I pull back. And I was crawling back. And then I got up. And I start-- and I felt it, that she turned around because I heard a commotion of the Polish girl, which I respected them that they gave me enough time to-- to get away. And then they started screaming. So the [GERMAN] turn around, and I know that she grabbed the [INAUDIBLE]. By that time, I picked up the cabbage and I throw it. Because there were a lot of girls front of the kitchen. They were waiting either for the [HUNGARIAN] or for the bread. And I didn't care who catches it, but I didn't want the cabbage to be on me. In the meantime, I mingled with the other girls so she couldn't see who-- where I am.

speaker: interviewee
text: And whom do you think caught the cabbage? My sister Clara. I didn't want to believe it. I said, this is a miracle. I don't want to believe it. One day, my younger sister went to the kitchen, front of the kitchen, because they brought in a truckload of potatoes. So she went there. And she stole-- you know, she ran over and she stole a few of them. Who caught her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Elsa Koch. The blond with the beautiful face and with the-- how they call it. I don't know the name of it. But you have like a bun. Bun. She started to hit her. Some of the girls who knew us, they came to me, and they said, you can say goodbye to your sister. Because Elsa Koch wouldn't left her go by. I started crying. I said, my god. We suffered so much until now, and here she is caught. So she was taken into one of the-- the storage barrack. And she started to hit her with the whip, you know, like a dance. And the meantime, there was a interpreter. We called her Piano Leg.

speaker: interviewee
text: And most likely, she was the most beautiful person you want to encounter. But she was like some kind of a [? laufer ?], or how you call it, who delivers papers to the Blockaltestes and takes them back and so on. So she was there. And Elsa Koch said to her, ask her why did she steal-- steal the potatoes? So she asked my sister. She said, because I was hungry. So she said, you couldn't be hungry. You people are getting food. You are a pig. And then again another whip. And again another whip. And she said, which barrack are you? My sister said 12, because she knew that the children supposed to be in the block 12. So she said, if you are in block 12, you get enough food. You-- you-- you fed beautifully. So she kept on hitting her. And meantime, this Piano Leg woman kept on talking to her. And between the conversation, she-- she said it under her nose in Hungarian while she-- the-- Elsa Koch was talking. In Hungarian, she was saying, run away, you stupid idiot. And then my sister ran out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she was waiting when she would shoot after her. But I guess she-- Elsa Koch figured that she will die anyway, so why-- why waste a bullet. This was one occasion. Another occasion, I noticed that one of these girl whom we were together in the ghetto, in Kosice, whose brother disappeared, she is working in the kitchen. So I told my older sister, Clara. I said, take off your dress. And I put it on myself. I said, I'm going over to the kitchen, and I'm going to tell Irene. Because Irene was a beautiful girl with blonde hair, and she spoke perfect Jewish. So I go over. I'm waiting. I'm waiting until she comes out, because they took the-- some kind of a garbage. I don't know what garbage, because they cooked the garbage anyway for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she was taking. I ran over to her. She said, get away from me. Get away from me. I said, look. I said, I have this extra dress on me. I said, I could make you a-- an apron. I said, but I want you to give me something. Some potatoes or some cabbage or something.

speaker: interviewee
text: She said, tomorrow. So I said, but don't forget. She said, no. Get away, get away, get away, get away. So I went home. I said to my sister, here is your dress. Put it on. And home. I went back to the barrack, and the-- she put the dress on.

speaker: interviewee
text: So next day, I go and I told her that we are sewing it. We are working on it. And I said, do you have anything? She says, I'm going to bring out later. So I was sitting. I had all the time. I mean, I wasn't going anywhere except counting the lice the way they are walking on the-- on the earth. So I waited. And sure enough, she came out. She had like a basket, something. And she throw the cabbage [? down ?]. I picked it up. I put it between my leg. And I was holding it with the dress, because otherwise if they see that I'm carrying a cabbage, they would kill me. They would kill me before I even have a chance. So I got home. I cut it up with the knife in the dark. And we kept on eating it. And then some of the girls said, I hear somebody is making noise. What are you eating? Give me some. Give me some. I said, I'm not eating anything. What can I give you? So it was very-- you have to be very, very careful. And I had heard while I was in Auschwitz that there was this doctor's wife who was part of Romania, and her husband was working with Mengele. And she was carrying-- Do you know what the name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. So she was carrying packages from her husband. Can you imagine? In the [GERMAN]. She had salami. She had beautiful bread. And she had marmalade. She had a lot of different things. And some of the kapos who were bringing in the main workers, they were the one who were bringing it in for her. And when I met some of my friend here in United States and I told her what I had heard, she said, I know, Magda. She said, I come from the same area. And I told her that she has to share the food with us because otherwise God will punish her. And you know, all kinds of [INAUDIBLE] mindset that she was telling her. And this woman was sharing the food with this friend of mine.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's a small world.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- yeah. And so what happened is that that was one occasion. And there was times when we were standing on line front of the kitchen, and this truckload of-- the truck was passing by. We didn't know what's in the truck. And I see that this person, a woman, is wrapped in a blanket. And she is moving and trying to roll off the back of the truck. Because whoever it was in a hurry to collect all of these people, they didn't put up the back of the truck. So she managed-- she maneuvered to roll off the truck. And she with a dump. She get on the-- the middle of the street. And then she was maneuvering herself with the blanket that she rolled on to the gutter, to the-- and I said to myself that how life is, that you want to live. She doesn't have the strength to get up. She doesn't have the strength to do anything. But she wants to live. For how long, a minute longer, a half a minute longer. When I had seen that, I said I want to live. I want to tell the world what goes on here. With the intelligent, the high class, educated and-- and the-- the-- the high powered Germans, that they are the-- above all. And they could do this to us, that we felt that we are at the bottom of the pit. We are-- we are-- we are worse off than a dog. We are worse off than any animal. Because when you take in a dog, you take it in because you want to take care of it. But we were herded into this [GERMAN]. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That we didn't see any future. But I said if this woman who had not be able to stand on her feet, and she wants to live. I want to live to tell the world what is going on here. What happening. So that's why I'm really happy in a way that I had an opportunity to tell the honest, unfortunate, heartbreaking, painful story to you, which is really in a nutshell. Because when you were in Auschwitz, every minute was a year. And every day was 10 years. It got to the point where we were-- like couldn't even concentrate from the brom that we have taken in. In the very beginning, we were like zombies. We-- we couldn't think. After suffering in the train and then in the ghetto, on the train, getting off, selecting every day, every day, nighttime.

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- it got to the point already where we were hoping for a real miracle. And we said-- we were always looking up. And we said, God, if we are the chosen people, why do you let us suffer? Why do we have to go through all this? This is worse than hell. Hell must be wonderful comparing to this place. So one day, like towards middle of or beginning of November, we had heard a big bust. The big blow. And we didn't know what it was. But meantime, we had seen some airplanes flying by. And we were always hoping they would throw a bomb and then it would finish us, because it was just unbearable by that time. So one day, they selected us. And when they selected us-- All three of you.

speaker: interviewee
text: All four of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, with your cousin.

speaker: interviewee
text: All four of us. So then we felt that must be a good place because they locked up the door on the barrack. And there was the Lola, the Lageralteste. There was the four Blockalteste who was with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said to my sister Clara that we must be going in a good place because otherwise they would know. They wouldn't be there. So it was no more that you have to go to the kitchen with the [HUNGARIAN] to get the food. Somehow they brought it in, and they bought us potatoes. Cooked potatoes. Boiled potatoes. So this German [GERMAN] was the one who was throwing it up on every [POLISH]. And as many people were there, that's how many people had to share the potatoes. And unfortunately, we were on the top because I didn't want all that lice to fall on me, and all the cysts to come on me. So I always liked to go on the top.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the-- the potatoes fell right between the slit because of nothing. So maybe I would have like one or two potatoes left over. So next day when we were getting the potatoes again, so I put out my dress that she should put the potatoes on it. She gave me a bang on my head. I thought it's going to fell off. And she throw the potatoes on the-- on the [POLISH]. So vicious, you know, but so vicious that it-- it's just unbearable.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you find out why they were feeding you differently?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The only thing I know that they-- we weren't allowed to go out from the barrack. But one day when they were taking us to the lavatory, to the bathroom, I had seen that there were-- like the hurricane hit that [GERMAN]. There were-- there were empty spots. No people are walking around in the-- in the [GERMAN]. And there was this covered truck. And in the covered truck, there were a lot of girls inside. And I seen one of the girl climbed up from the back, climbed up on top of the truck, and from the truck jumped on top of the barrack. And when some of the Germans had seen her, they just shot her like a dog. Like a dog. She just rolled down, and that was that. So I don't think they were taking these girls to work. Because those girls wouldn't-- wouldn't try to escape, and they wouldn't try to go up on top of the barrack.

speaker: interviewee
text: So after that, we were staying inside in the barrack. And one day, we were told that we should lined up. And we lined up. And we were standing front of the gate. So I didn't want to be left behind because I know when it's a good transfer, everybody wants to be on it. So I was pushing myself to be in the front line. So-- I'm afraid I have to interrupt for a tape change.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I will continue.
